text:
The way a stranger holds a door open for someone they cannot yet see — that gesture interests me more than almost anything else in the catalogue of human goodness. There is no audience for it in the moment it begins. The person pushing that door open does not yet know if anyone is coming; they have simply felt the pull of an obligation to a hypothetical other, and responded to it. What moves me is that interval: the door held ajar for a stranger who may not materialize, the arm extended into empty air on behalf of a possibility.

We tend to celebrate kindness in proportion to its cost — the heroic sacrifice, the dramatic intervention — as though virtue were measured by the size of the wound it leaves in our comfort. But small kindnesses reveal something different and arguably stranger: that decency is not always a decision. It can be more like a reflex that has been cultivated so long it operates below deliberation, the way a musician's fingers know the chord before the mind names it. The held door is moral muscle memory, the distillate of a thousand small choices that have gradually become no choice at all.

And here is what the held door holds: it treats the invisible as worthy of attention. The approaching stranger, still too far away to be seen clearly, still a silhouette or a footstep, is already being accommodated, already factored into someone's small reckoning with the world. That is a form of imagination doing the work of ethics — reaching forward in time to meet a person before they arrive, deciding in advance that they matter.

This is worth noticing because it runs exactly counter to the logic of efficiency, which says: act only when the outcome is certain. Small kindnesses are performed on the basis of hope rather than evidence. They assume, quietly, that someone is coming. That assumption is itself a kind of love.
